core_identity.title: Relative Frequency: Experimental Probability with Dice
core_identity.author: Gerry Docherty

curriculum_alignment.subject: mathematics
curriculum_alignment.subtopic: relative frequency and convergence to theoretical probability
curriculum_alignment.seeds_of_change: Calculate relative frequency from repeated trials and explain why, as trials increase, experimental results tend to get closer to the theoretical probability.

verse_ality.driving_question: What happens to relative frequency as the number of trials increases, and how does it compare to the theoretical probability?

knowing_world.focus: Explore experimental probability using relative frequency, and investigate how repeated trials make outcomes settle closer to the theoretical probability (e.g., 1/6 for a fair die).
tools: Interactive: Relative Frequency Dice Roll Web App (canva_embed): https://www.canva.com/design/DAG6W23DLRw/lo_7-BcLpoCQzZa0ULXalg/view?embed

human_story.focus: Explore how probability and risk inform decisions in healthcare, climate science, insurance, and justice, including how biased data or flawed tools can lead to unfair outcomes.
justice_prompts: How can experimental probability help us challenge assumptions or make decisions based on evidence?

expressive_self.focus: Use metaphor and reflection to connect uncertainty, fairness, and expectation to your own experiences while building a clearer intuition for randomness.
activities: Create a metaphor or sketch for probability (e.g., balancing scales, a spinning wheel, a wave).
In your own words, explain how you feel when the dice roll doesn’t go the way you expected. What do you think is “fair”?

designed_world.focus: Use an interactive dice tool to run repeated trials, build a frequency/relative frequency table, and compare results across sample sizes and between people.
design_challenges: Use the interactive dice app to roll repeatedly and record outcomes for 10, 20, 30, and up to 100 rolls.
Calculate relative frequency for a chosen outcome (or each outcome) and observe how it changes as trials increase.
Compare your results with a partner. Did you both get similar relative frequencies? Why or why not?
Ethical design: How would you redesign this tool to support someone who cannot use a mouse or who sees colours differently?

regulation_supports: Pause: check in with how you feel when outcomes are uncertain.
Movement: tap your hand six times (one for each face) and notice the rhythm.
Breathing: breathe in for “Try” and out for “Observe” for six rolls; notice how breathing helps you focus on patterns instead of outcomes.

pathways.name: Final Task: Experimental Probability Report
pathways.focus: Run repeated dice trials, calculate frequencies and relative frequencies, describe what changes as the number of rolls increases, and propose one accessibility improvement for the tool.
products: Completed frequency and relative frequency table (from the interactive tool).
Short reflection: “What did I notice as the number of rolls increased?”
One idea to improve or redesign the dice experiment for accessibility.
criteria: Conceptual Understanding – Explain why relative frequency changes and why it tends toward the theoretical probability with more trials.
Fluency & Accuracy – Correctly calculate and update frequencies and relative frequencies.
Application to Problems – Use the data to draw meaningful conclusions about fairness and randomness.
Independence & Reflection – Reflect thoughtfully and propose a realistic accessibility improvement.

required: Division to calculate a ratio (frequency ÷ trials)

builds_toward: Relative frequency graphs and larger sample investigations
Risk, bias, and data ethics in real-world decision-making
